policy_statement: Develop requirements and digital tools for reporting climate data from suppliers in connection with procurement (2024–2030).
mechanism_description: Develop reporting requirements and digital tools so suppliers provide climate data during procurement, improving emissions transparency to support lower-emissions purchasing decisions.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement governance (data disclosure) → emissions transparency/traceability → purchasing shifts to lower-GHG options → ↓ supply-chain (Scope 3) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Public (or institutional) procurement processes and supplier engagement are modified to require standardized climate data disclosure (e.g., product carbon footprints, EPDs, corporate inventories) supported by digital reporting tools and workflows.  
2) Mediating process: Improved measurement, reporting, and comparability reduces information asymmetry and enables emissions-based evaluation criteria, benchmarking, and accountability that influence contract awards and supplier practices.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Procurement decisions shift demand toward lower-emissions products/services and incentivize suppliers to decarbonize, leading to reduced upstream and embodied GHG emissions associated with purchased goods and services.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that emissions transparency in procurement enables substitution toward lower-GHG goods/services and suppliers, reducing lifecycle (Scope 3) procurement emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core mechanism is information and governance enabling lower-carbon procurement choices that reduce lifecycle GHG emissions (Mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “Resource Efficiency” could apply if the policy primarily reduced material/energy use, here the central lever is emissions disclosure to drive lower-GHG purchasing, which most directly targets emissions reductions. It is not primarily about hazard reduction (Adaptation) or ecosystem functions (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If reporting is implemented without any linkage to purchasing criteria or supplier selection, the mitigation effect may be indirect/delayed; however, the mechanism as stated (“to support lower-emissions purchasing decisions”) keeps the dominant intent and effect mitigation-oriented.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy matching this mechanism—supplier climate data reporting requirements/tools for procurement transparency—has its main climate relevance through enabling emissions-based procurement decisions and associated GHG reductions, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether impacts fall mainly on embodied carbon, operational emissions of procured services, or broader supplier decarbonization (and how strongly) depends on policy design (thresholds, verification, weighting), which must be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented purely as voluntary reporting or for internal tracking with no decision-use, near-term emissions reductions may be minimal; conversely, if paired with explicit low-carbon purchasing mandates, the mitigation impact becomes more direct. Potential rebound/greenwashing risks exist if data quality is low or unverified.
key_indicators: “Develop requirements”, “digital tools”, “reporting climate data”, “from suppliers”, “in connection with procurement”
co_benefits: Improved supplier transparency and data governance; potential efficiency gains in procurement processes.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a procurement requirement if the “requirements” are embedded as mandatory tender/contract clauses; however, the core action described is establishing reporting/disclosure requirements and tools, so information-disclosure is the best fit.
